species: Southern Resident Killer Whale (Complex Whistles + Clicks)
sound_type: Complex whistles + echolocation clicks. Whistles: tonally pure, 1-18 kHz, flexible, short-range social, signature-developing. Clicks: broadband 10-80+ kHz, echolocation, prey detection, navigation. E=8.
ecosystem: San Juan Channel -- body of water between San Juan Island and Lopez/Shaw Islands. Connects Haro Strait to Strait of Juan de Fuca. Critical summer Chinook salmon foraging habitat for Southern Residents.
season: Resident (year-round core range). San Juan Channel critical July-September (peak Chinook salmon passage).

coordinates.description: San Juan Channel / Lime Kiln Point State Park, San Juan Island. Most critical shore-based SRKW viewing location. Center for Whale Research base: Friday Harbor, San Juan Island.

taxonomy_note.description: Second consecutive SRKW entry (after degree 270 pod call). Degree 271 focuses on whistles (short-range social, flexible, signature-developing) and clicks (echolocation, prey detection, sensory navigation). Together with degree 270 (discrete calls), degrees 270-271 document the complete three-system SRKW acoustic portrait. ENDANGERED, ~73-75 individuals, J/K/L pods, obligate Chinook salmon, ESA 2005.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: See degree 270. Orcinus orca: 'killer of the underworld.'
taxonomy_note.genus_note: See degree 270. Second entry for Orcinus orca.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Odontoceti. Echolocation is the defining sensory system of all toothed whales. The SRKW echolocation system is among the most sophisticated studied.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Delphinidae continues for second consecutive degree (270-271). Two consecutive SRKW entries: pod call identity (270) and whistles/clicks (271).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Whistles: 1,000-18,000 Hz. Clicks: 10,000-80,000+ Hz (broadband). Combined acoustic output of an active SRKW pod spans essentially the full range of bioacoustic significance.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Whistles: 3,000-12,000 Hz. Clicks: 20,000-60,000 Hz (echolocation peak frequency).
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Whistles: typically 1,000-18,000 Hz, tonally pure or slightly FM. Clicks: broadband pulses, peak 20,000-60,000 Hz, individual click duration ~100-200 microseconds.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual whistles: 0.5-6 seconds. Individual clicks: ~100-200 microseconds. Click trains during foraging: continuous over minutes. Full foraging session acoustics: hours.
song_metrics.note_structure: WHISTLES: Smooth frequency contours, ascending, descending, or complex arched/looped. Individually variable. Signature whistle is individually distinctive. CLICKS: Rapid broadband pulses in trains; rate increases as prey proximity decreases (approach phase). Codas: patterned click series used in social context.
song_metrics.note_types: Whistles (social, short-range), clicks (echolocation/sensory), codas (patterned click social use), plus combinations and transitions between categories.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Whistle bouts between close individuals. Click train: initial search phase (slow rate, long interval), approach phase (accelerating rate), final phase (very rapid, very short interval). Multiple individuals may click simultaneously during cooperative foraging.
song_metrics.timbre: Whistles: smooth, flute-like, tonally pure -- arguably the most 'musical' SRKW vocalization. Clicks: sharp, rapid, broadband -- sensory rather than tonal. The contrast between whistle and click timbres is dramatic.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=8 -- Maximum. Whistle amplitude: moderate (short-range by design). Click amplitude: very high at source; focused directional beam through melon. Click train detectable by hydrophone at long range; most energy in foraging beam directed at prey.
song_metrics.tempo: Whistles: variable, socially context-dependent. Clicks: slow during search, rapid during approach (interclick interval inversely proportional to target distance). Arrival click rate: 500+ per second at close range.
song_metrics.context: WHISTLES: Affiliative, short-range, social bonding, mother-calf, signature ID. CLICKS: Navigation, prey detection, cooperative foraging coordination. Both systems operate simultaneously with discrete calls during active foraging.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Signature whistles: individually variable (individual identity). Pod whistles: may have some pod-level characteristics. Echolocation clicks: relatively consistent across individuals within population.
song_metrics.key_property: ORCA ARC (2 OF 7). SRKW second consecutive entry. Complete three-system acoustic portrait: discrete calls (270) + whistles + clicks (271). ENDANGERED, ~73-75 individuals. San Juan Channel critical habitat. Center for Whale Research (Balcomb 1976). Echolocation at 10,000-80,000+ Hz: seeing with sound. Signature whistles: individual identity in acoustic form. Boat noise reduces echolocation effectiveness: the acoustic disturbance that directly impairs foraging.

plumage.overall: See degree 270. No variation. Black and white countershading, white eye patch, individual saddle patches.

nodes.title: Whistles -- Signature and Social
nodes.description: Tonally pure, 1-18 kHz, flexible, individually variable. Signature whistles as individual identifiers. Mother-calf contact maintenance. Short-range affiliative communication. The SRKW's most expressive acoustic channel.

nodes.title: Echolocation -- Seeing With Sound
nodes.description: Broadband 10,000-80,000+ Hz. Focused through melon, received through mandibular fat. Detects Chinook at 100-200m+. Discriminates species, size, orientation. Click rate accelerates during prey approach. Boat noise reduces effective range.

nodes.title: Three-System Acoustic Portrait Complete
nodes.description: Degrees 270-271: discrete calls (culturally transmitted, pod-specific) + whistles (short-range social, signature) + clicks (echolocation, sensory). Complete portrait of SRKW acoustic communication across two degrees.

nodes.title: San Juan Island Research Community
nodes.description: Center for Whale Research (Balcomb 1976-present): longest running wild cetacean population study. Lime Kiln Point hydrophones. Orca Network. NOAA NWFSC. The most studied wild cetacean population on earth.

cross_references.species: Puget Sound Orca S01 Pod Call
cross_references.relationship: CONSECUTIVE SRKW. Degree 270: discrete calls (cultural dialect, pod identity). Degree 271: whistles (short-range social) + clicks (echolocation). Together = complete acoustic portrait.
ECOTYPE CONTRAST. Southern Resident: continuously vocal, salmon-eating. Bigg's (transient): silent during hunting, mammal-eating. Same genus, opposite strategies.
